m_Item1.QuestionText:
void main ( ) 
    {
struct
     {
         int v ;
    } k;
   
    (& k)->v=200;
    printf("%d", k.v);
    }
m_Item1.Explanation: &k is nothin but address of structure ( pointer to struct) and we have assign 200 to v variable so k.v will print 200.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the program?

typedef struct data;
{
int x;
sdata *b;
}sdata
m_Item1.Explanation:
As c code is compiling line by line as there is error in 1st line it will give message  Error: in typedef .

error: expected identifier or ‘(’ before ‘{’ token. as ; is given after stuct data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C, structure values can be passed as arguments to function by?

Options.OptionText: Passing each member of the structure as an argument of function call.
Passing copy of the entire structure to the called function.
Passing structure as an argument using pointer.

m_Item1.Explanation: Structure can be passed to a function as variable or pointer or even individual structure members.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of following C code?
#include <stdio.h>
void main()
{
   struct emp
   {
     char name[20];
     int age;
     float sal;
   };
   struct emp e ={"Tiger"};
   printf("%d %f",e.age,e.sal);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: If first member in structure is initialized using array notation, rest of the members are automatically initialized to zero or null depending on datatype.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following comment about the usage of structures is true?

Options.OptionText: Storage class can be assigned to individual member.
Individual members can be initialized within a structure type declaration.
The scope of the member name is confined to the particular structure, within which it is defined.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Storage classes can not be assigned to individual members in structure.
Individual members can not be initialized in structre.
Scope of structure members are restricted to structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
About structure which of the following is true:
1.Structure members are aligned in memory depending on their data type.
2.The size of a structure may not be equal to the sum of the size of its members.
m_Item1.Explanation:
In C/C++ structures are used as data pack. It doesn’t provide any data encapsulation or data hiding features.
Because of the alignment requirements of various data types, every member of structure should be naturally aligned. The members of structure allocated sequentially increasing order.

as seen from the perspective of size of the structure:
|| int | int | int | int | float | float | float | float | char | pad | pad | pad ||

here each '| |' represents a byte of the memory assigned to the structure.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following structure.
struct numname
{
  int no;
  char name[25];
};
struct numname n1[] = {
  {12, "Raja"},
  {15, "Selvan"},
  {18, "Prema"},
  {21, "Naveen"}
};
The output of the following statement would be:
printf("%d, %d",n1[2].no, ( *( n1 + 2)).no);
m_Item1.Explanation: Third member of the structure array can be accessed using subscript as n1[2] and pointer notation as  *(n1+2). so n1[2].no and ( *( n1 + 2)).no  returns 1st  element of  3rd array of structure.
